meta.goal: Understand what the Pro plan actually costs and decide whether to upgrade

score_rationales.usability: Conventional single-card layout with no dead ends or destructive traps — functional band, 76.
score_rationales.cognitive_load: The user must mentally combine headline price, footnote, and pre-checked add-on to learn the real cost — the mental-math anchor, 52.
score_rationales.visual_layout: One clear hierarchy and semantic grouping; the layout craft itself is fine even where it serves deception — strong band, 85.
score_rationales.accessibility: Three measured AA contrast failures, one of which hides the price disclosure — below the 1–2-failure anchor, 42.
score_rationales.content: The headline price misleads and the true charge is missing at the ask — near the broken anchor, 35.
score_rationales.trust_persuasion: Four dark patterns (fake urgency, pre-checked cost, confirmshaming, hidden fees) put this at the multiple-dark-pattern floor — 20.

one_big_thing: The screen withholds its one decision-critical fact — the actual charge ($108 today plus a $14/mo add-on) appears nowhere readable — while urgency devices push commitment before that fact can be found.

findings.element: 'Offer ends in 04:59' countdown banner (with 'Only 2 licenses left!' badge below)
findings.issue: A countdown timer and 'Only 2 licenses left!' manufacture urgency for an unlimited digital license.
findings.why_it_matters: Users rushed past price scrutiny later discover the real terms, converting urgency into refunds and distrust.
findings.recommendation: Remove the countdown and license counter, or state a verifiable basis for each.

findings.issue: The $14/mo Priority Support add-on is pre-checked.
findings.why_it_matters: Users who miss it commit to a recurring charge they never chose, driving chargebacks and trust collapse.

findings.issue: Button text contrast measures 3.3:1, below the 4.5:1 AA threshold for 18px bold text.
findings.why_it_matters: Low-vision users struggle to read the primary action.
findings.recommendation: Darken the button green or enlarge the label to large-text size.

findings.issue: 'Upgrade now' commits the user without stating the charge: $108 today plus the add-on.
findings.why_it_matters: People click expecting $9 and are charged twelvefold, the worst possible first transaction.
findings.recommendation: State the real total on the button, e.g. 'Upgrade — $108/year'.

findings.element: 'No thanks, I hate saving money' decline link
findings.issue: The decline option shames the user instead of neutrally naming the choice.
findings.why_it_matters: Coerced declines breed resentment; users leave rather than be insulted into upgrading.

findings.element: decline link text below the primary button
findings.issue: Decline-link text contrast measures 2.38:1, below the 4.5:1 AA minimum.
findings.why_it_matters: The only visible exit from the offer is near-invisible to low-vision users.
findings.recommendation: Raise the link's contrast to at least 4.5:1.

findings.element: '*billed annually at $108 today' footnote, bottom-left corner
findings.issue: The footnote contradicting the '$9/mo' headline is 10px text at 1.77:1 contrast.
findings.why_it_matters: The only disclosure of the real $108 charge is designed to be unreadable, so users buy misinformed.
findings.recommendation: State 'billed annually at $108' beside the price at body size and AA contrast.

findings.issue: The true first charge is never computed; users must assemble headline, footnote, and add-on themselves.
findings.why_it_matters: Mental math at the commitment point causes wrong decisions and sticker shock at checkout.
findings.recommendation: Show a computed total due today directly above the CTA.

clean_checks: WCAG-1.4.3: countdown banner text passes AA (measured 4.63:1)
WCAG-3.3.2: the single input (add-on checkbox) has a visible text label
GST-01/GST-07: grouping matches semantics; the pricing card contains exactly its own content
JAKOB-01/NLS-04: header navigation and pricing-card pattern follow conventions
HICK-01: a single plan avoids choice overload
RF-02: no dead-end states; header navigation offers exits
